findings.title: All circuit and causal claims rest on one 2B base model plus five steering pairs pre-selected for steering success, while the front door generalizes to 'Medical AI'
findings.target: index.html H1 ('Medical AI must be ready to interpret them') and lede ('rewriting the sentence in clinical terms restores it'); methods.html closing claim 'the circuit view shows where that difference arises'; translation/index.html titration note 'the effect is the clinical circuit, not steering itself'; preregistration_tierB.md 'the mechanism ... is causally manipulable with placebo control'
findings.argument: Every attribution graph, feature tag, lens readout, patch, and steering result comes from gemma-2-2b — the only model Neuronpedia can trace with a transcoder set (engine CLAUDE.md; other registry ids 400/500 server-side). The causal-faithfulness evidence is thinner than the page admits: data/provenance.json steering_titration is explicitly computed 'on the 5 boost-recovered downgrade cases', i.e. the dose-response cells (4/5, 5/5, 4/5) are measured on pairs selected because boosting recovered them — near-ceiling recovery is guaranteed by construction, and the translation page's tt-note presents these numbers without disclosing the selection rule. The only informative contrasts are the n=5 placebo (0/5) and n=5 rank arm. Meanwhile the site's vocabulary inflates a next-token probe of a 2024 base model into medical-AI advice claims: index.html calls the top-k spread 'competing recommendations', methods.html says 'The advice changed, not just its probability', translation says 'Translation matters most when it changes the advice' — directly contradicting the project's own scope statement that it 'measures language-model probabilities only — nothing here generates or evaluates medical advice'. The methods Limitations section discloses the one-model scope, but the H1, og tags, and per-page claims do not inherit the caveat.
findings.fix: Scope the headline and og/meta descriptions to the measured object ('in small open language models' / 'in gemma-2-2b'); replace 'recommendations'/'advice' with 'predicted next word' in the four places it appears; add the selection rule to the titration caption ('pairs pre-selected as boost-recovered; the informative contrasts are the placebo and rank arms, n=5 each') and re-run the dose-response on a fresh, unselected downgrade sample before keeping 'the effect is the clinical circuit' language.

findings.title: The clinical_mass / feature-tagging construct has zero reported validation and a circular category definition
findings.target: methods.html Step 3 ('Every feature gets sorted: clinical, off-target, or structural'; 'the clinical share of the circuit falls and the off-target share rises'), the off-target definition ('These are the mechanism of the language penalty'), and wording-differences/index.html Fig. 1 caption ('green clinical features stack over the swapped word and feed the continuation; the idiom replaces them')
findings.argument: Every green-vs-dark story on the site depends on tags produced by keyword-matching machine-written auto-interp descriptions of Gemma Scope transcoder features. No page or data file reports any validation of this tagger: no human-audited sample, no inter-rater agreement, no precision/recall, no sensitivity of the headline clinical-share deltas to the keyword vocabulary. The Limitations bullet concedes 'both steps can be wrong, and a mislabeled feature shifts the clinical-mass numbers' but never quantifies how much. Worse, the taxonomy is circular: 'off-target' is *defined* as 'features dragged in by colloquial wording ... the mechanism of the language penalty' — the category presupposes the causal conclusion the figures are offered to demonstrate. The project already knows the metric is fragile to tagging coverage (untagged models yield clinical_mass ~0.0 as a documented artifact), yet the tagged-model numbers are presented with no analogous artifact analysis.
findings.fix: Publish a validation appendix: a stratified random sample of ~100 features from landed traces, tagged blind by two humans, with agreement stats and precision/recall against the keyword tagger; re-run the headline clinical-share deltas under perturbed/ablated keyword lists and report the range; rewrite the off-target definition descriptively ('features responding to idiom, register, and non-medical senses') and let the mass-shift result, not the definition, carry the mechanism claim.

findings.title: 'Never formed' means 'never entered a top-8 readout window', but the page repeatedly claims nonexistence — and the instrument's own clinical-side data show the window misses answers that demonstrably exist
findings.target: technical/index.html Part 2 ('Did the right answer ever exist inside the model?'), Part 3 dd-form-text ('The difference between the wordings is not timing. It is existence.'), and the 'never formed' class definition ('the answer is not readable at any layer')
findings.argument: The lens records the target only when it enters the per-layer top-8 (jlens_insights.json: 'ranks are within the lens's top-8 readout', prefix match MIN_PREFIX_CHARS=4 per Amendment 2). Rank-censoring at 8 cannot distinguish 'the answer never existed' from 'the answer sat at rank 9–20 with substantial probability'. The site's own data quantify the window's blindness: 40 of 170 *clinical* readings are classed never-formed even though those targets pass the >=0.02 measurability screen and are, in several pairs, readable on the patient side (4 'held' pairs have clin_formed=null). An instrument with a demonstrated ~24% miss rate on answers known to have measurable probability cannot support existence claims; the honest disclosure exists only in the page's endnote while the body text of Parts 2–3 makes the strong claim three times.
findings.fix: Record per-layer target log-probability (or full rank) instead of top-8 membership if the hosted API allows; if it does not, say so as an instrument ceiling and rewrite the body text to 'never entered the readable window (top-8)' wherever 'never formed'/'existence' appears; add a window-size sensitivity check (class counts at top-4/8/16) to jlens_insights.json and cite it in the caption.

findings.title: The capture/hijack taxonomy attributes to wording what is mostly lens blindness: 34 of 56 'captures' are unreadable under BOTH wordings
findings.target: technical/index.html Part 2 ('in a capture, the everyday reading owns the trajectory from the start') and Part 3 dd-tax-text ('56 captures ... Most failure here is capture, not hijack: the wording decides the computation early')
findings.argument: In data/jlens_insights.json, 34 of the 56 pairs classed 'capture' have clin_formed=null as well — the clinical answer never enters the window even under clinical wording. For those pairs the everyday wording cannot be said to have 'owned' or 'decided' anything; the lens simply cannot see the target under either phrasing (weak target, tokenization/prefix-match failure, or rank>8 throughout). The wording-attributable capture count is 22, versus 14 hijacks — a 22 v 14 split, not the 56 v 14 the page narrates. The same paragraph also self-contradicts: it claims capture 'decides the computation early' while reporting capture winner lock-in at median layer 24 of 25. Finally, 'formed' has no persistence criterion: the showcased hijack exemplar's patient-side trace is rank 7 at layer 20, gone at 21, rank 8 at 22, rank 5 at 23, gone after — single-layer blips at the censoring threshold count as formation, so hijack counts are inflated by jitter at the top-8 boundary.
findings.fix: Restrict capture/hijack classification to pairs whose clinical-side reading is lens-readable (clin_formed non-null); report the 34 both-null pairs as a separate 'unreadable to the lens under both wordings' class; add a persistence rule (e.g., in-window for >=2 consecutive layers or reaching rank <= 4) before 'formed' is assigned; republish the counts and rewrite dd-tax-text around the corrected 22 v 14 comparison, dropping 'decides the computation early' or reconciling it with the late lock-in medians.

findings.title: Translation-page 'restores the prediction / restores the circuit' claims outrun an uncontrolled n=14, and the depth-router story is contradicted by the site's own flat by-class recovery numbers
findings.target: translation/index.html subtitle ('Translating the patient sentence back into clinical terms restores the prediction'), og:description ('restores the circuit'), the depth claim ('answers that form and are lost late, which translation recovers, and answers that never form, which translation supplies'), and technical/index.html 'Read the failure, pick the fix' router
findings.argument: The classified evidence is 18 cases from one downgrade-selected set on one model (provenance.json: 8 recovered, 3 unrecovered, 3 worsened, 4 already-ok) — a 57% recovery rate among 14 classifiable cases, presented under a universal present-tense headline. There is no control arm: the placebo-paraphrase comparison is listed as 'queued' on the technical page, and because the translated panel runs only on flagged downgrades (per the preregistration), regression to the mean alone predicts some apparent recovery. The site's own router data undercut the routing story: jlens_depth.json by_class shows mean recovery of +0.157 tiers for lost-late (n=4), +0.163 for never-formed (n=10), and +0.13 for *retained* pairs that had nothing to fix (n=21) — recovery is flat across depth classes, the never-formed class currently recovers slightly MORE than lost-late (opposite to draft-Amendment-2's H-D1 prediction), and the retained-class +0.13 looks like the noise/regression floor. 'Restores the circuit' (og tag) is a circuit-level claim supported only by one exemplar's qualitative third panel. And the flagship number — recovery to 41–45%, *above* the 26% clinical baseline — is presented as strength when it is evidence the rewrite is not an equivalence-preserving restoration but a different, stronger prompt. Methods' own audit ('translating patient words into clinical words destroys information') is never confronted on this page.
findings.fix: Rewrite the subtitle and og:description to the measured rate ('restored the top clinical continuation in 8 of 14 classifiable downgrade cases; 3 worsened'); delete 'restores the circuit' until clinical-mass recovery is quantified across cases; run the queued placebo-paraphrase arm before any depth-routing language returns; display the retained-class +0.13 beside the router table as an explicit noise benchmark and state that current by-class means do not yet separate; flag the above-baseline recovery as a prompt-nonequivalence caveat.

findings.title: Part 4's cross-model evidence is presented as more independent than it is, and the same-host aliasing disclosure is never reconciled with the -it row two scroll-lengths above it
findings.target: technical/index.html Part 4 ('Eight models, one measurement', 'across four model families', the claim-grade table's gemma-2-2b-it row, limitations' 'medgemma-4b-it ... still shows a below-zero penalty') and Part 3 'Does tuning move the depth?' (fig-it)
findings.argument: Parts 1–3 correctly hold back the lens tuning comparison because gemma-2-2b and gemma-2-2b-it return byte-identical hosted readouts on all 31 pairs, 'consistent with the hosted service resolving both ids to the same model'. But Part 4 on the same page lists gemma-2-2b-it as one of eight models with a penalty nearly double the base model's (−6.2pp vs −3.5pp, model_stats.json) and never tells the reader these rows come from a different backend (CPU inference on real -it weights) — which, if stated, would itself resolve the aliasing question. A skimming reader can take fig-it's 'every pair identical' as a substantive finding that tuning does not move depth; the figure has zero informational content by the page's own account. Independence is also overstated: base/-it and the two qwen sizes are counted separately among the 'eight models', medgemma-4b-it is a gemma derivative, all models read overlapping Claude-authored stimuli, and BH correction across 8 non-independent tests does not make the replications independent. The medgemma sentence in methods' limitations leans on a knife-edge interval (CI [−6.9, −0.2] pp, asymmetry q=0.075, n=119).
findings.fix: Add one sentence to Part 4: 'the gemma-2-2b-it row is CPU-measured on the released -it weights; its differing penalty is why the identical hosted lens readouts above are treated as an id-aliasing artifact.' Replace fig-it with that text note until Neuronpedia answers. State that the eight models span four families with shared stimuli and report a family-clustered or family-level summary alongside the per-model tests; quote medgemma's CI wherever it is cited as evidence about medically tuned models.
